synopsis: Kevin Mitnick draws on his background as a former hacker to show how governments, corporations, and criminals track individuals' digital activity without their knowledge. The book provides step-by-step techniques for protecting personal data, from password hygiene and Wi-Fi security to advanced methods for achieving online anonymity.

sources.title: The Art of Invisibility | Hachette Book Group

synopsis: Greg Nuckols and Omar Isuf present a framework for strength training that emphasizes individual variation, purposeful programming, and ongoing self-experimentation over rigid prescriptions. The book argues that effective lifting requires aligning training goals, program design, and personal feedback to build lasting progress.

Adrian Slywotzky presents 23 distinct business models through a Socratic dialogue between a mentor and protégé, each chapter examining a different strategy by which companies generate and sustain profit. The book teaches how to analyze competitive dynamics and identify the structural choices that determine which organizations thrive financially.

sources.title: The Art of Profitability | Hachette Book Group

synopsis: David Spiegelhalter uses real-world problems—from the Harold Shipman murders to ovarian cancer screening—to guide readers through the principles statisticians use to extract knowledge from data. The book covers how to frame questions, assess assumptions, and correctly interpret results in an era defined by the proliferation of data.

sources.title: The Art of Statistics | Hachette Book Group

synopsis: David Spiegelhalter examines how probability and statistical reasoning can help people think more clearly about risk, chance, and unknowns encountered in medicine, climate science, and everyday decision-making. The book explores how to update beliefs in light of new evidence and how to communicate uncertainty honestly.

sources.title: The Art of Uncertainty | W. W. Norton & Company

synopsis: Written by Chinese military strategist Sun Tzu around the fifth century BCE, this thirteen-chapter text lays out principles of warfare centered on deception, adaptability, intelligence, and the strategic use of terrain and timing. Its framework for gaining advantage over adversaries has been applied across military, political, and business contexts for centuries.

Jacob Bronowski traces the development of human civilization as a story of scientific and intellectual discovery, moving from stone tools and agriculture through geometry, genetics, and relativity. Originally a companion to his BBC television series, the book frames scientific progress as the defining expression of humanity's capacity to understand and shape the natural world.

Niall Ferguson chronicles the history of financial systems from ancient credit markets and bond trading through the rise of joint-stock companies, insurance, and globalized capital. He argues that financial innovation has shaped historical events as profoundly as scientific or military developments, tracing how money's ascent underpins civilization's progress and crises.

sources.title: The Ascent of Money | Penguin Random House

synopsis: A geneticist in Jakarta discovers a treatment that rewrites the understanding of human evolution, while a compromised intelligence agent uncovers a shadowy organization called the Immari that believes it holds the key to humanity's survival against a spreading virus. The thriller weaves together ancient conspiracy, genetic experimentation, and questions about the true origins of the human species.

Written in pieces between 1771 and 1790 and addressed originally to his son, Benjamin Franklin's autobiography recounts his rise from a Boston printer's apprentice to a central figure in American civic, scientific, and political life. It details his self-improvement methods, business ventures, civic projects, and evolving philosophical views on virtue, religion, and public service.

sources.title: The Autobiography of Benjamin Franklin | SparkNotes

synopsis: Darwin's autobiography, written for his family and first published in complete form in 1959, covers his education, the voyage of the Beagle, and the development of his thinking on evolution by natural selection. It also reflects candidly on his working methods, his views on religion and philosophy, and the personal relationships that shaped his intellectual life.

sources.title: The Autobiography of Charles Darwin | Wikipedia

synopsis: Nassim Nicholas Taleb presents a collection of philosophical and practical aphorisms that challenge modern civilization's tendency to distort reality in order to fit preconceived models, from redefining intelligence to suit standardized tests to inventing ailments to sell treatments. Part of the Incerto series, the book uses the ancient aphoristic form to probe themes of uncertainty, knowledge, and human hubris.

sources.title: The Bed of Procrustes | Penguin Random House

synopsis: Physicist David Deutsch argues that good explanations—those that are hard to vary while still accounting for the phenomena—are the engine of all human progress, from science and politics to art and moral philosophy. The book contends that the capacity to generate and improve explanations is potentially unlimited, making the scope of future human achievement boundless.

sources.title: The Beginning of Infinity | Penguin Random House

synopsis: Earl Swift chronicles the decades-long political, engineering, and social struggle behind the creation of the American interstate highway system, profiling the largely forgotten planners and visionaries who shaped it long before Eisenhower signed the Federal Aid Highway Act of 1956. The book also examines how the highways displaced thousands of urban residents and transformed the physical and cultural landscape of the country.

Michael Malone traces Silicon Valley's history from the birth of the semiconductor in the 1930s through the explosive growth of the personal computer era, drawing on interviews with figures including William Shockley and Steve Jobs. The book documents the ambition, competition, and darker forces—espionage, grueling working conditions, betrayal—that drove the Valley's transformation from a cluster of garages into a global industry.

William Bernstein identifies four conditions—secure property rights, scientific rationalism, functioning capital markets, and reliable transport and communication—as the prerequisites that converged in early nineteenth-century Europe to ignite sustained economic growth. The book examines why prosperity arrived so late in human history and why its spread has remained uneven across the globe.

Saifedean Ammous surveys the history of money from ancient commodity systems and precious metals through the gold standard and modern central banking, arguing that each transition involved a loss of monetary soundness. The book then presents Bitcoin as a decentralized, fixed-supply alternative capable of restoring the properties of sound money that government-issued currencies lack.

Bill Bryson surveys the human body system by system—from the brain and skeleton to the immune system and gut—explaining how each part functions, how scientists came to understand it, and what can go wrong. The book combines biological detail with the history of medicine to convey the complexity and improbable resilience of the body's many interconnected processes.

sources.title: The Body: A Guide for Occupants | Wikipedia

synopsis: Mary Childs follows Bill Gross from his early career as a card-counting gambler through his founding of PIMCO and his rise as the most powerful figure in the bond market, and on to his eventual fall. The book uses Gross's story to explain how the bond market became central to modern finance and how one investor's strategies reshaped the entire financial system.
